cards.front: What is the mechanism of action of ACE inhibitors?
cards.back: Block conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II by inhibiting angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE). This reduces aldosterone secretion and decreases blood pressure.
cards.notes: Examples: Lisinopril, Enalapril, Captopril

cards.front: What are the classic findings of nephrotic syndrome?
cards.back: Massive proteinuria (>3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, lipiduria, and generalized edema (anasarca).
cards.notes: Most common cause in children: Minimal Change Disease. In adults: Membranous Nephropathy.

cards.front: What enzyme is deficient in Phenylketonuria (PKU)?
cards.back: Phenylalanine hydroxylase (PAH), which converts phenylalanine to tyrosine. Alternatively, deficiency of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4) cofactor.
cards.notes: Autosomal recessive. Musty body odor. Intellectual disability if untreated.

cards.front: What is the difference between MHC Class I and MHC Class II molecules?
cards.back: MHC I: Found on all nucleated cells; presents intracellular (endogenous) antigens to CD8+ T cells. MHC II: Found on antigen-presenting cells (macrophages, dendritic cells, B cells); presents extracellular (exogenous) antigens to CD4+ T cells.
cards.notes: MHC I = 1 chain + β2-microglobulin. MHC II = 2 chains (α + β).

cards.front: What are the stages of the cell cycle?
cards.back: G1 (growth/preparation) → S (DNA synthesis) → G2 (preparation for mitosis) → M (mitosis). G0 is the quiescent phase for non-dividing cells.
cards.notes: Cyclin-CDK complexes regulate progression. Rb is a key G1/S checkpoint regulator.

cards.front: What is the Virchow triad for thrombosis?
cards.back: 1) Endothelial injury (most important), 2) Stasis or turbulent blood flow, 3) Hypercoagulability of blood.
cards.notes: Named after Rudolf Virchow. Key concept for DVT, PE, and stroke risk assessment.

cards.front: What is the mechanism of action of statins?
cards.back: Competitively inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in cholesterol synthesis. This upregulates hepatic LDL receptors, increasing LDL clearance from blood.
cards.notes: Side effects: hepatotoxicity, myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. Examples: Atorvastatin, Rosuvastatin.

cards.front: What are the layers of the epidermis from deep to superficial?
cards.back: Stratum Basale → Stratum Spinosum → Stratum Granulosum → Stratum Lucidum (only in thick skin) → Stratum Corneum.
cards.notes: Mnemonic: Californians Like Girls in String Bikinis (superficial to deep)

cards.front: What is the pathophysiology of Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus?
cards.back: Autoimmune destruction of pancreatic β-cells in the islets of Langerhans, leading to absolute insulin deficiency. Associated with HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR4.
cards.notes: Presents with polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, weight loss. Risk of DKA.

cards.front: What are the 4 classic signs of inflammation?
cards.back: 1) Rubor (redness), 2) Calor (heat), 3) Tumor (swelling), 4) Dolor (pain). A 5th sign: Functio laesa (loss of function) was added later.
cards.notes: First 4 described by Celsus. 5th by Virchow. Mediated by histamine, prostaglandins, bradykinin.

cards.front: What is the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation?
cards.back: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]), where [A⁻] is the conjugate base and [HA] is the weak acid. For bicarbonate buffer: pH = 6.1 + log([HCO₃⁻] / (0.03 × PCO₂)).
cards.notes: Normal arterial pH: 7.35–7.45. Normal HCO₃⁻: 22–26 mEq/L. Normal PCO₂: 35–45 mmHg.

cards.front: What cranial nerve is responsible for the gag reflex?
cards.back: Afferent (sensory) limb: CN IX (Glossopharyngeal). Efferent (motor) limb: CN X (Vagus).
cards.notes: Loss of gag reflex may indicate brainstem lesion or CN IX/X damage.

cards.front: What are the branches of the aortic arch from right to left?
cards.back: 1) Brachiocephalic trunk (divides into right common carotid and right subclavian), 2) Left common carotid artery, 3) Left subclavian artery.
cards.notes: Variant: bovine arch — left common carotid arises from brachiocephalic trunk.

cards.front: What is the function of p53 tumor suppressor gene?
cards.back: Guardian of the genome. Activates DNA repair, arrests cell cycle at G1/S checkpoint, and induces apoptosis if DNA damage is irreparable. Encoded on chromosome 17.
cards.notes: Li-Fraumeni syndrome: inherited p53 mutation → multiple cancers at young age. Most commonly mutated gene in human cancers.

cards.front: What are the consequences of a posterior cerebral artery (PCA) stroke?
cards.back: Contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing (occipital lobe involvement). May also cause alexia without agraphia if dominant hemisphere involved.
cards.notes: PCA supplies: occipital lobe, medial temporal lobe, thalamus, midbrain.

cards.front: What is the difference between apoptosis and necrosis?
cards.back: Apoptosis: Programmed cell death; energy-dependent; cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, apoptotic bodies; no inflammation. Necrosis: Pathologic cell death; cell swelling, membrane disruption, enzymatic digestion; triggers inflammation.
cards.notes: Apoptosis uses caspases. Intrinsic (mitochondrial) and extrinsic (death receptor) pathways.

cards.front: What are the classic ECG findings in a myocardial infarction?
cards.back: Hyperacute: tall peaked T waves. Acute: ST elevation (STEMI). Hours-days: T wave inversion, pathological Q waves. Weeks: Q waves may persist as evidence of infarction.
cards.notes: STEMI criteria: ≥1mm ST elevation in 2 contiguous leads (≥2mm in V1-V3).

cards.front: What organisms are encapsulated bacteria, and why does this matter?
cards.back: SHiNE SKiS: Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, Neisseria meningitidis, Escherichia coli (K1), Salmonella, Klebsiella, Group B Strep. Capsule inhibits phagocytosis; requires opsonization by antibodies and complement.
cards.notes: Asplenic patients are at high risk. Vaccinate against S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis.

cards.front: What are the features of Cushing syndrome?
cards.back: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, abdominal striae, hyperglycemia, hypertension, muscle wasting, osteoporosis, immunosuppression, and skin thinning.
cards.notes: Most common cause: Exogenous corticosteroids. Most common endogenous cause: Cushing disease (pituitary adenoma secreting ACTH).

cards.front: What is the complement system's classical vs alternative pathway?
cards.back: Classical: Activated by antigen-antibody complexes (IgG, IgM) → C1 → C4 → C2 → C3. Alternative: Activated by microbial surfaces directly → C3 spontaneous hydrolysis → Factor B, D. Both converge at C3 → C5 → MAC (C5b-9).
cards.notes: C3a/C5a: anaphylatoxins. C3b: opsonization. C5b-9: membrane attack complex.
